### Ayan Bhattacharjee

#### Academic Advisor & Program Coordinator | Driving Growth in Educational Services | Helping Institutions Expand & Develop Successfully

 Published Apr 8, 2025 

[+ Follow](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=%2Fpulse%2Fusing-ai-tool-railway-signaling-paving-way-safer-ayan-bhattacharjee-u875c&trk=article-ssr-frontend-pulse_publisher-author-card)

The global railway industry is undergoing a transformation—and artificial intelligence (AI) is driving the change at full speed. Among the various railway subsystems embracing innovation, railway signaling stands out as one of the most critical domains where AI's potential can be game-changing.

From optimizing train movements to predicting failures before they occur, AI is not just a buzzword in signaling—it's becoming a vital tool for improving safety, efficiency, and capacity. Let’s explore how AI is revolutionizing this space and what it means for railway professionals, infrastructure managers, and passengers alike.

* * *

### 🚉 What is Railway Signaling?

In essence, railway signaling is the system that controls train traffic, prevents collisions, and ensures that trains move safely and efficiently along the tracks. Traditional signaling systems involve block sections, interlockings, and train detection equipment such as track circuits or axle counters. While these systems have evolved over decades, they often rely on pre-set rules and static logic.

Enter AI.

* * *

### 🧠 How AI Enhances Railway Signaling

1.   Predictive Maintenance of Signaling Assets
2.   Dynamic Route Optimization
3.   Anomaly Detection & Intrusion Alerts
4.   Enhanced Decision Support for Signaling Operators
5.   Simulation & Testing
6.   Interoperability & Data Harmonization

* * *

### 🌍 Real-World Applications

*   Deutsche Bahn is using AI to optimize timetables and signal planning for better punctuality.
*   Indian Railways has piloted AI-based surveillance for level crossing safety and intrusion detection.
*   Network Rail (UK) is leveraging AI to predict failures in point machines and track circuits.
*   China Railway uses AI for real-time traffic management in high-speed rail corridors.

* * *

### 📈 Benefits of AI in Railway Signaling

Benefit Impact 🔒 Improved Safety Early detection of faults and real-time alerts reduce accidents and failures. ⏱️ Better Punctuality AI minimizes delays by adjusting routes and reducing headways. 🛠️ Lower Maintenance Costs Predictive maintenance avoids costly breakdowns and emergency repairs. 🌱 Energy Efficiency AI helps optimize train acceleration and deceleration, reducing energy use. 📊 Enhanced Capacity Smarter signaling allows more trains on the same track infrastructure.

* * *

### 🤔 Challenges and Considerations

As with any technology shift, integrating AI into signaling systems brings its own set of challenges:

*   Data Quality & Availability: AI models are only as good as the data they receive.
*   Cybersecurity: AI integration must ensure robust protection against hacking and sabotage.
*   Human-AI Interaction: Operators must be trained to trust and validate AI recommendations.
*   Regulatory Compliance: Signaling changes must pass rigorous safety certifications.

* * *

### 👷♂️ The Human Factor: Adapting Roles in the AI Era

Railway professionals must now upskill in data analytics, AI basics, and system integration. Signal engineers, in particular, will transition from logic designers to intelligent system integrators—those who understand both legacy logic and modern AI-driven tools.

* * *

### 🚀 Looking Ahead

AI in railway signaling is not about replacing human expertise, but about augmenting it with intelligent tools that can process vast data and offer actionable insights in real time. As the global demand for high-speed, safe, and sustainable rail travel rises, AI will be a cornerstone of next-generation signaling systems.

# Artificial Intelligence: The future of rail mobility

![](/sites/alstom.com/files/styles/cover_hero_mobile/public/2024/09/10/ServicesAnalytics_Mainline%20video%20%281%29.jpg?h=8f74817f&itok=SK3_5dzc)

Highlights
----------

*   •A comprehensive survey on the state-of-the-art applications of AI in railways in a wide spectrum. 
*   •In-depth statistics and distributions of the surveyed papers are provided. 
*   •Gaps are identified and future directions are discussed. 

Abstract
--------

Nowadays it is widely accepted that [Artificial Intelligence](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/artificial-intelligence "Learn more about Artificial Intelligence from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (AI) is significantly influencing a large number of domains, including railways. In this paper, we present a [systematic literature review](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/systematic-review "Learn more about systematic literature review from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of the current state-of-the-art of AI in [railway transport](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/railway-transport "Learn more about railway transport from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). In particular, we analysed and discussed papers from a holistic railway perspective, covering sub-domains such as maintenance and inspection, planning and management, safety and security, [autonomous driving](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/autonomous-driving "Learn more about autonomous driving from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and control, [revenue management](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/revenue-management "Learn more about revenue management from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), transport policy, and passenger mobility. This review makes an initial step towards shaping the role of AI in future railways and provides a summary of the current focuses of AI research connected to rail transport. We reviewed about 139 scientific papers covering the period from 2010 to December 2020. We found that the major research efforts have been put in AI for rail maintenance and inspection, while very limited or no research has been found on AI for rail transport policy and revenue management. The remaining sub-domains received mild to moderate attention. [AI applications](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/artificial-intelligence-applications "Learn more about AI applications from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are promising and tend to act as a game-changer in tackling multiple railway challenges. However, at the moment, AI research in railways is still mostly at its early stages. Future research can be expected towards developing advanced combined AI applications (e.g. with optimization), using AI in [decision making](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/decision-making "Learn more about decision making from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), dealing with uncertainty and tackling newly rising cybersecurity challenges.

1. Introduction
---------------

[Artificial Intelligence](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/artificial-intelligence "Learn more about Artificial Intelligence from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (AI) is defined as a [computerized system](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/computerized-system "Learn more about computerized system from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") that is able to perform physical tasks and cognitive functions, solve various problems, or make decisions without explicit human instructions([Kaplan and Haenlein, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0968090X22001206#b77)). Nowadays, AI has become one of the most important areas of research in almost all fields in academia and industry. Among many other sectors where mature applications of AI are flourishing, we notice that AI is still largely at its infancy stage in the [railway sector](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/railway-sector "Learn more about railway sector from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Nonetheless, emerging evidence has begun to show the potential of AI in railways and suggests that AI can play important roles such as optimizing complex [railway systems](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/railway-system "Learn more about railway systems from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), detecting infrastructure faults/defects, improving safety and security of urban rail networks and enhancing customer service quality([Burroughs, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0968090X22001206#b16)). In addition, from a holistic point of view,[Gibert et al. (2017)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0968090X22001206#b53) expect that AI will soon become a common tool used throughout the rail industry. The impact of AI on the railway sector is thus supposed to be pivotal, as there is evidence showing AI may completely revolutionize how certain railway areas operate, such as capacity management, [life cycle cost](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/life-cycle-cost "Learn more about life cycle cost from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), maintenance, passenger flow prediction, and significantly reduce errors from both humans and computers, high-level automation and auto-adaptive systems. Recent developments of the applications of AI to railway have already emerged. For instance, Toshiba has built an AI system that can enhance the train timetabling for Greater Anglia, a train operator in the UK([Fragnelli and Sanguineti, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0968090X22001206#b45)). The French operator SNCF already uses an AI-based [predictive maintenance](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/predictive-maintenance "Learn more about predictive maintenance from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") on Transilien commuter trains in the Paris region([SNCF, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0968090X22001206#b154)).

A primary definition associates AI to any machines acting in a way that seems intelligent([Przegalinska, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0968090X22001206#b125)) or exhibiting characteristics that are typical of human reasoning. This definition suffers from the lack of a universally accepted definition of “intelligence”. Later a new definition was developed based on the famous Turing Test([Turing, 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0968090X22001206#b165)): a machine is deemed intelligent if it is indistinguishable from a human during an interaction with an impartial observer. Over the years, more structured and detailed definitions have been introduced, e.g.[European Commission, 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0968090X22001206#b34), [European Commission and Joint Research Centre, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0968090X22001206#b35) and[Copeland (2019)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0968090X22001206#b26). These definitions try to capture the broad nature of AI and its potential coverage. For certain domains, such definitions may be too abstract and thus are less likely to be widely accepted. Overall, such general definitions tend to reduce the uptake leading to no common agreement on what AI actually represents. To address this challenge, we have proposed the following definition of AI in[Bešinović et al. (2021)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0968090X22001206#b12), which is suitable to support next-generation [railway transport](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/railway-transport "Learn more about railway transport from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and [traffic engineering](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/traffic-engineering "Learn more about traffic engineering from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"): _AI is the discipline gathering all the aspects that allow an entity to determine how to perform a task and/or make a decision based on the experience matured by observing samples and/or by interacting with an environment, possibly competing against or cooperating with other entities_. This definition is based on the need to emphasize the following aspects that are crucial when considering [AI application](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/artificial-intelligence-applications "Learn more about AI application from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in the railway domain: (1) Being able to learn from examples (i.e.not coded to solve a task by a programmer), (2) Possibly operating in an environment populated by other entities, (3) Accomplishing a task that would require intelligence if done by a human, (4) Not include a trivial [automatism](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/automatism "Learn more about automatism from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and (5) Can be both hardware or software (or a hybrid). What stated so far allow us to also highlight the relationship between some of the most spread AI-related concepts including, for example, [Deep Learning](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/deep-learning "Learn more about Deep Learning from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (DL), [Machine Learning](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/machine-learning "Learn more about Machine Learning from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (ML), and the AI itself. We would like to clarify that Machine Learning is a subarea within AI addressing the techniques that are able to learn from examples and improve with experience, without being explicitly programmed for a given task. Within ML, [Artificial Neural Networks](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/artificial-neural-network "Learn more about Artificial Neural Networks from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (ANNs) are inspired by the structure of human [brain](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/protocerebrum "Learn more about brain from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"): an ANN has a layered structure composed of [interconnected neurons](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/interconnected-neuron "Learn more about interconnected neurons from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (i.e.,perceptrons), which interact with each other to perform a given task. In recent years, the technological evolution allowed engineers to build increasingly complex ANN, such as the so-called [Deep Neural Networks](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/deep-neural-network "Learn more about Deep Neural Networks from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (DNN), which are a class of ANNs with several layers of neurons, allowing them to automatically extract features from data. DNN enable [Deep Learning](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/deep-learning "Learn more about Deep Learning from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), which “allows [computational models](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/computational-modeling "Learn more about computational models from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") that are composed of multiple processing layers to learn representations of data with multiple levels of abstraction”([LeCun et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0968090X22001206#b87)).

In[Bešinović et al. (2021)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0968090X22001206#b12), we have proposed a systematic taxonomy of AI based on the above definition where we divide various branches and study areas of AI into several [General AI](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/artificial-general-intelligence "Learn more about General AI from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") Fields including (i) [Expert systems](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/expert-system "Learn more about Expert systems from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"); (ii) [Data Mining](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/data-mining "Learn more about Data Mining from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"); (iii) Pattern Recognition; (iv) Adversarial Search; (v) Evolutionary Computing; (vi) Machine Learning; (vii) Operations Research and Scheduling; (viii) Logic Programming; (ix) [Natural Language Processing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/natural-language-processing "Learn more about Natural Language Processing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")& Speech Recognition; (x) [Computer Vision](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/computervision "Learn more about Computer Vision from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")&[Image Processing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/image-processing "Learn more about Image Processing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"); (xi) [Autonomous Systems](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/autonomous-system "Learn more about Autonomous Systems from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")& Robotics. These general AI fields will be used throughout this paper. The readers can refer to[Bešinović et al. (2021)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0968090X22001206#b12) and[RAILS D1.1 (2020)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0968090X22001206#b129) for more details in the definition and taxonomy of AI in the railway context.

There are a number of review papers in the literature dedicated to the application of AI in railways. However, all of these surveys tend to focus on a specific aspect of the combination of AI and railway sub-domains. For instance, in[Ghofrani et al. (2018)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0968090X22001206#b50), a review of the recent [applications of big data](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/application-of-big-data "Learn more about applications of big data from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") analytics in the context of railway engineering and transportation was conducted. A survey of the existing and potential use of AI in railway assets was provided in[Jain and Yogesh (2019)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0968090X22001206#b70). In[Wen et al. (2019)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0968090X22001206#b178), a review and appraisal of data-driven approaches applied in train dispatching management was given, where data-driven approaches are grouped into [statistical methods](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/methods-statistical "Learn more about statistical methods from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [graphical models](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/graphical-model "Learn more about graphical models from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and ML.[Chenariyan Nakhaee et al. (2019)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0968090X22001206#b23) gave a survey on recent applications of ML in rail track maintenance. The authors provided a taxonomy in classifying the existing literature. In addition, they presented the shortcomings of current techniques and discussed certain remedies for the research community and rail industry.[Liu et al. (2020)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0968090X22001206#b98) gave a review of applications of [visual inspection](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/visual-inspection "Learn more about visual inspection from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") technology based on image processing in the [railway industry](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/railway-industry "Learn more about railway industry from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). In[Xie et al. (2020)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0968090X22001206#b182), urban flow prediction from [spatiotemporal data](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/spatiotemporal-data "Learn more about spatiotemporal data from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") was systematically reviewed and existing urban flow prediction methods based on ML were overviewed with some discussions on the difficulties and some ideas in this direction. A review on railway system resilience was presented in[Bešinović (2020)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0968090X22001206#b11) focusing on [quantitative approaches](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/quantitative-approach "Learn more about quantitative approaches from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). The review discussed resilience metrics and approaches, where the latter contains data-driven and optimization-based approaches. Several rising future scientific topics were identified as well.[Corman and Meng (2015)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0968090X22001206#b27) presented a survey of the recent approaches on online railway traffic rescheduling problems, including both dynamic and stochastic aspects; the authors concluded by stating that optimization-based models were the most used approaches. So far, the literature in this field of AI and railways lacks a holistic overview, which desirably would, on one side, take a broad perspective of AI, and on the other side, consider railway system as a whole, i.e.cross-map with various sub-domains of the railway sector. According to what we have investigated in the peer’s review work as well as the expected outcomes we want to achieve in this study, we summarized the review papers we mentioned before and made a comparison among them in [Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0968090X22001206#tbl1).

To understand the current position of AI as a whole in railways, this paper reviews the state-of-the-art research studies to recognize the current worldwide focuses and results with the aim of determining a variety of targeted AI applications for railway systems and analysing their patterns and distributions. Given the vastness of the rail sector, we holistically subdivided it into seven sub-domains in order to carry out a more structured review and describe different complementary aspects of railway system. Then, we separately analysed the literature related to the different sub-domains to identify promising future research directions to facilitate further uptake of AI by academia and industry. enriched_points.expanded_content: AI enhances railway signaling by processing vast amounts of data, optimizing train schedules, predicting maintenance needs, and improving safety through real-time decision-making.
IoT devices in railway systems enable seamless communication between trains and infrastructure, facilitating data collection for improved monitoring, operational efficiency, and passenger experience.

Leveraging real-time analytics allows railway operators to make informed decisions quickly, enhancing operational efficiency, reducing delays, and improving safety measures through proactive responses.
